title: The Harbour Trail
summary: A narrator recounts a long walk along Sydney's harbour trail, taking in the water views, the other walkers, and reflecting on how city life and nature can surprisingly coexist.
body: I had been planning this walk for months, and on a clear Tuesday morning in October I finally set off along the harbour trail. The path stretches for several kilometres along the edge of the water, winding through coves, past rock platforms, and up onto sandstone headlands that look out over one of the most famous stretches of coastline in the world. The first thing that struck me was the light. Sydney's October sun has a particular quality — bright but not yet harsh — and it made the water sparkle in a way that felt almost unreal. Sailing boats moved slowly in the distance. The opera house sat perfectly still on the opposite shore, its white shells catching the morning glow. I had seen it a hundred times in photographs, but standing there with the salt air on my face, it felt different. More modest, somehow, and more beautiful. The trail attracts all kinds of people. Within the first twenty minutes I had passed a group of elderly women doing their morning walk at a brisk pace, a young couple who appeared to have stopped for an argument that they were trying to keep quiet, and a man in his fifties with enormous headphones who was almost running. A child was crouched at the edge of a rock pool, absolutely absorbed in whatever she had found. What I find remarkable about urban trails like this one is the way they create a temporary truce between the city and the natural world. The sandstone cliffs have been here for millions of years. The harbour is ancient. But all around them, within sight and earshot, is a modern city of five million people — the traffic, the cranes, the glass towers. And yet, on the trail, none of that feels dominant. The trees close in overhead, the path narrows, and for a few minutes you could almost believe you were somewhere completely remote. I stopped at a bench near a small lookout and ate the sandwich I had brought. Below me, two kayakers paddled out from a cove. A cormorant stood on a rock with its wings spread wide, drying them in the sun. I stayed there for longer than I meant to. There is something about being near water that slows the mind. By the time I reached the end of the trail I had walked for nearly three hours. My legs were tired but my head felt clear. Cities often get a bad reputation for being relentless and disconnecting. But days like this remind me that within almost every large city, if you know where to look, there are pockets of genuine calm. The harbour trail is one of Sydney's great gifts to itself.

annotations.meaningVi: bắt đầu hành trình, lên đường
annotations.exampleEn: We set off early to avoid the traffic.

annotations.meaningVi: uốn lượn qua, chạy ngoắt ngéo qua
annotations.exampleEn: The river winds through the valley.

annotations.meaningVi: gây ấn tượng với tôi, khiến tôi chú ý
annotations.exampleEn: What struck me first was the silence.

annotations.meaningVi: gần như không thật, như trong mơ
annotations.exampleEn: The sunset was almost unreal — deep orange and purple.
I love the smell of salt air near the ocean.

annotations.meaningVi: với tốc độ nhanh, khỏe khoắn
annotations.exampleEn: He walked at a brisk pace to stay warm.

annotations.meaningVi: đắm chìm vào, mải mê với
annotations.exampleEn: She was absorbed in her book and didn't hear me.

annotations.meaningVi: thỏa thuận tạm thời, sự đình chiến tạm thời
annotations.exampleEn: The two sides agreed to a temporary truce.

annotations.meaningVi: trong tầm nhìn và tầm nghe
annotations.exampleEn: Stay within sight and earshot of the group.

annotations.meaningVi: bao phủ bên trên, trùm lên phía trên
annotations.exampleEn: The forest closes in overhead on the narrow path.

annotations.meaningVi: hẻo lánh, xa xôi, tách biệt
annotations.exampleEn: The village is so remote that few tourists ever visit.
I stayed longer than I meant to and missed the bus.

annotations.meaningVi: làm chậm tâm trí, giúp đầu óc thư thái
annotations.exampleEn: Meditation slows the mind and reduces stress.

annotations.meaningVi: tiếng xấu, danh tiếng tiêu cực
annotations.exampleEn: That neighbourhood has a bad reputation, but it's actually quite safe.
The relentless noise of the city exhausted him.

annotations.meaningVi: những khu vực nhỏ, những khoảnh
annotations.exampleEn: There are pockets of poverty even in wealthy cities.
After the storm, a genuine calm settled over the village.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect — Describing actions completed before the main narrative moment
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở đầu 'I had been planning this walk for months' dùng thì quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn để cho thấy hành động kéo dài từ trước đó cho đến một thời điểm quá khứ cụ thể. Thì quá khứ hoàn thành đơn ('I had seen it a hundred times') diễn đạt hành động đã hoàn thành trước thời điểm kể chuyện. Cấu trúc: had + past participle.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening sentence 'I had been planning this walk for months' uses the past perfect continuous to show an action ongoing up to a past moment. The past perfect simple ('I had seen it a hundred times') signals an action completed before the narrative moment. Structure: had + past participle.

grammarNotes.title: Participle Phrases — Adding description efficiently
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài văn dùng nhiều cụm phân từ hiện tại để thêm mô tả mà không cần câu phức. Ví dụ: 'winding through coves' (thay vì 'which winds through coves'), 'looking out over…', 'catching the morning glow'. Cấu trúc này tạo nhịp điệu mượt mà và chuyên nghiệp hơn so với câu ghép thông thường.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses present participle phrases to add description without full relative clauses. For example, 'winding through coves' (instead of 'which winds through coves'), 'looking out over…', 'catching the morning glow'. This creates a smoother, more sophisticated rhythm than using repeated relative clauses.

translationVi:
Tôi đã lên kế hoạch cho chuyến đi bộ này trong nhiều tháng, và vào một buổi sáng thứ Ba quang đãng tháng Mười, tôi cuối cùng cũng lên đường dọc theo đường mòn ven cảng. Con đường trải dài vài kilômét dọc theo mép nước, uốn lượn qua những vịnh nhỏ, qua những bãi đá, và leo lên những mỏm đất sa thạch nhìn ra một trong những đường bờ biển nổi tiếng nhất thế giới.

Điều đầu tiên gây ấn tượng với tôi là ánh sáng. Mặt trời tháng Mười của Sydney có một chất lượng đặc biệt — sáng nhưng chưa gay gắt — và nó khiến mặt nước lấp lánh theo cách gần như không thật. Những chiếc thuyền buồm di chuyển chậm chạp ở phía xa. Nhà hát Opera đứng yên hoàn toàn ở bờ đối diện, những vỏ sò trắng của nó bắt ánh sáng ban mai. Tôi đã thấy nó hàng trăm lần trong ảnh, nhưng đứng ở đó với không khí mặn trên khuôn mặt, cảm giác lại khác. Khiêm nhường hơn, bằng cách nào đó, và đẹp hơn.

Đường mòn thu hút đủ loại người. Trong hai mươi phút đầu, tôi đã đi qua một nhóm phụ nữ cao tuổi đang tản bộ buổi sáng với bước đi nhanh nhẹn, một cặp đôi trẻ có vẻ đang cãi nhau nhưng cố giữ im lặng, và một người đàn ông khoảng năm mươi tuổi với đôi tai nghe khổng lồ đang gần như chạy. Một đứa trẻ đang cúi xuống bên mép hồ đá, hoàn toàn đắm chìm vào thứ gì đó nó vừa tìm thấy.

Điều tôi thấy đáng kinh ngạc về những đường mòn đô thị như thế này là cách chúng tạo ra một sự đình chiến tạm thời giữa thành phố và thế giới tự nhiên. Những vách đá sa thạch đã tồn tại ở đây hàng triệu năm. Cảng biển thì cổ xưa. Nhưng xung quanh chúng, trong tầm nhìn và tầm nghe, là một thành phố hiện đại của năm triệu người — tiếng ồn giao thông, cần cẩu, những tòa tháp kính. Nhưng trên đường mòn, không có gì trong số đó cảm thấy chiếm thế thượng phong. Những hàng cây khép tán ở trên đầu, con đường thu hẹp lại, và trong vài phút bạn gần như có thể tin rằng mình đang ở một nơi hoàn toàn hẻo lánh.

Tôi dừng lại ở một chiếc ghế gần một điểm nhìn nhỏ và ăn chiếc bánh sandwich tôi mang theo. Phía dưới tôi, hai người chèo thuyền kayak chèo ra từ một vịnh nhỏ. Một con chim cốc đứng trên tảng đá với đôi cánh giang rộng, phơi nắng. Tôi ở đó lâu hơn dự định. Có điều gì đó trong việc ở gần mặt nước làm chậm tâm trí.

Đến khi tôi đến cuối đường mòn, tôi đã đi bộ gần ba tiếng. Chân tôi mỏi nhưng đầu óc cảm thấy thông thoáng. Các thành phố thường bị mang tiếng xấu là không ngừng nghỉ và xa cách. Nhưng những ngày như thế này nhắc nhở tôi rằng trong hầu hết các thành phố lớn, nếu bạn biết tìm ở đâu, luôn có những khoảnh bình yên thực sự. Đường mòn ven cảng là một trong những món quà tuyệt vời mà Sydney tặng cho chính mình.

questions.prompt: When did the narrator finally begin the harbour trail walk?
options: On a rainy Friday morning in September
On a clear Tuesday morning in October
On a Sunday afternoon in November
On a warm Wednesday evening in October
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'on a clear Tuesday morning in October I finally set off along the harbour trail.'
questions.hint: The narrator mentions the day, time of day, and month.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe Sydney's October sun?
options: Harsh and blinding, difficult to enjoy
Weak and filtered through cloud cover
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Sydney's October sun has a particular quality — bright but not yet harsh.'
questions.hint: The narrator uses an em-dash to elaborate on the quality.

questions.prompt: What surprised the narrator about seeing the Opera House in person?
options: It was much larger than expected
It felt more modest and more beautiful than in photos
It was difficult to see clearly from the trail
questions.explanation: The narrator says it 'felt different. More modest, somehow, and more beautiful.'
questions.hint: The narrator contrasts their in-person experience with seeing photos.

questions.prompt: Which of the following people did the narrator NOT pass on the trail?
options: A group of elderly women walking at a brisk pace
A teenager sitting on a bench reading
A child absorbed in a rock pool
questions.explanation: The passage mentions elderly women, a young couple, a man with headphones, and a child at a rock pool — but no teenager reading on a bench.
questions.hint: Go through each person mentioned in the passage carefully.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator mean by a 'temporary truce between the city and the natural world'?
options: The city has agreed to protect the trail from development
On the trail, nature and city feel coequal rather than one dominating the other
The city has temporarily closed the trail to vehicles
Wildlife and humans rarely come into contact on urban trails
questions.explanation: The narrator means that on the trail, despite the city being visible and audible, nature feels present and neither the city nor nature completely dominates.
questions.hint: A 'truce' is an agreement to stop fighting — what might the 'fight' between city and nature be?

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe the feeling of the trail when the trees close in overhead?
options: As if you could almost believe you were somewhere completely remote
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'the path narrows, and for a few minutes you could almost believe you were somewhere completely remote.'
questions.hint: Think about how the narrator describes the feeling of isolation despite being in a city.

questions.prompt: What was the cormorant doing when the narrator observed it?
options: Diving into the water to catch fish
Standing on a rock with its wings spread wide, drying them in the sun
Sitting on a boat near the cove
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'A cormorant stood on a rock with its wings spread wide, drying them in the sun.'
questions.hint: The narrator observes the bird from a bench near a lookout.

questions.prompt: How long did the narrator walk along the harbour trail in total?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'By the time I reached the end of the trail I had walked for nearly three hours.'
questions.hint: This information is given near the end of the passage.

questions.prompt: What effect does water have on the narrator, according to the passage?
options: It makes the narrator feel energised and motivated
It reminds the narrator of childhood holidays
It makes the narrator feel lonely
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'There is something about being near water that slows the mind.'
questions.hint: The narrator says this after staying at the bench longer than intended.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say about large cities at the end of the passage?
options: Cities are inherently harmful to mental health
Most cities lack any green spaces for relaxation
Within almost every large city, there are pockets of genuine calm if you know where to look
Sydney is unique among cities for having natural spaces
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes: 'within almost every large city, if you know where to look, there are pockets of genuine calm.'
questions.hint: The narrator acknowledges cities' bad reputation but then offers a counterpoint.

cloze.template: I had been planning this walk for months, and on a clear Tuesday morning in October I finally {{b1}} along the harbour trail. The path stretches for several kilometres along the edge of the water, {{b2}} through coves, past rock platforms, and up onto sandstone headlands that look out over one of the most famous stretches of coastline in the world. The first thing that {{b3}} me was the light. Sydney's October sun has a particular quality — bright but not yet harsh — and it made the water sparkle in a way that felt almost unreal. Sailing boats moved slowly in the distance. The opera house sat perfectly still on the opposite shore, its white shells catching the morning glow. I had seen it a hundred times in photographs, but standing there with the salt air on my face, it felt different. More modest, somehow, and more beautiful. The trail attracts all kinds of people. Within the first twenty minutes I had passed a group of elderly women doing their morning walk at a brisk {{b4}}, a young couple who appeared to have stopped for an argument that they were trying to keep quiet, and a man in his fifties with enormous headphones who was almost running. A child was crouched at the edge of a rock pool, absolutely {{b5}} in whatever she had found. What I find remarkable about urban trails like this one is the way they create a temporary truce between the city and the natural world. The sandstone cliffs have been here for millions of years. The harbour is ancient. But all around them, within sight and earshot, is a modern city of five million people — the traffic, the cranes, the glass towers. And yet, on the trail, none of that feels {{b6}}. The trees close in overhead, the path narrows, and for a few minutes you could almost believe you were somewhere completely {{b7}}. I stopped at a bench near a small lookout and ate the sandwich I had brought. Below me, two kayakers paddled out from a cove. A cormorant stood on a rock with its wings spread wide, drying them in the sun. I stayed there for longer than I {{b8}} to. There is something about being near water that {{b9}} the mind. By the time I reached the end of the trail I had walked for nearly three hours. My legs were tired but my head felt clear. Cities often get a bad reputation for being relentless and disconnecting. But days like this remind me that within almost every large city, if you know where to look, there are {{b10}} of genuine calm. The harbour trail is one of Sydney's great gifts to itself.

blanks.explanation: 'Set off' is the phrasal verb used in the passage, meaning to begin a journey.
'Winding through' means moving in a twisting, curving way through an area.
'Struck me' is the idiomatic expression used — it means to notice or be impressed by something suddenly.
'At a brisk pace' is a fixed collocation meaning walking quickly and energetically.
'Absorbed in' means completely engrossed in or fascinated by something.
The narrator says the city does not feel 'dominant' on the trail — meaning it does not overpower the natural setting.
'Remote' means far from human habitation or activity — perfect for describing the illusory isolation on the trail.
'Longer than I meant to' is the exact phrase — 'meant to' expresses original intention.
'Slows the mind' is the metaphorical expression used to describe water's calming effect on thoughts.
'Pockets of' is an idiomatic expression meaning small, isolated areas of something within a larger context.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator describes a 'temporary truce' between the city and nature on the harbour trail. Do you think modern cities have a responsibility to preserve natural spaces for their residents, or is nature simply something people should seek outside the city? How does access to nature affect quality of life in urban environments?
